evaluation_inputs.system_description: The CAAS is a common avionics system designed for a family of U.S. Army Special Operations helicopters (A/MH-6, MH-47, and MH-60). It aims to provide a single, open, scalable architecture to modernize avionics, replacing proprietary legacy systems. The system manages cockpit workload, enhances situational awareness ('aviate, navigate, and communicate'), and is built on a foundation of commercial standards to facilitate portability and third-party upgrades.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The CAAS was already well into development at the time of evaluation. Evaluators performed a pre-exercise review of architecture documentation to capture approaches.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How does the cyclic executive ensure real-time performance?
How is the system partitioned to ensure availability and safety?

hardware_view_description: Hardware is portable between platforms ('plug and play' architecture).
Applications are location-transparent and can migrate to other hardware for load balancing.
Every application is resident on every box.
runtime_constraints: Inviolable timing and memory walls between applications (POSIX partitioning).
Applications must not overrun processing time limits or memory allotments.

guaranteed_resources.sockets: Number of sockets is known and guaranteed

questions: Does the partitioning strategy successfully isolate failures?
Do layers effectively isolate high-level graphics services?
Does the client-server approach effectively decouple system parts?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Invalid data is entered by the pilot, and the system does not crash.
Invalid data comes from an actor on any bus, and the system does not crash.
When a 1.9-second power interruption occurs, the system will execute a warm boot and be fully operational in 2 seconds.
A loss of Doppler occurs, the pilot is notified, and the Doppler timer begins a countdown.
A partition fails, the rest of the processor continues working, and the system continues to function.
The data concentrator suffers battle damage, and all flight-critical information is still available.
The mission processor in the outboard MFD fails, and that display and the rest of the system continue to operate normally.

business_goals: Minimize systems acquisition and sustainment costs
Reduce the total ownership cost over the life of the avionics system
Modernize the system with minimal impact to Army Special Operations readiness
Ensure that new CAASs work well with other programs
technical_constraints: Large set of applicable standards (ARINC 661, POSIX, CORBA, IEEE P1386/P1386.1, OpenGL, DO 178B)
Must support multiple helicopter platforms (A/MH-6, MH-47, MH-60)

architectural_approaches.description: The system uses layers to isolate high-level services (e.g., graphics) and POSIX partitioning to create inviolable timing and memory walls between applications, ensuring fault isolation and safety.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Partitioning isolates failures so that one partition failure does not affect others, allowing the system to continue functioning.
Timing and memory walls prevent applications from interfering with each other, reducing cascading failures.

architectural_approaches.description: Applications use a client-server approach to decouple system components, enabling location transparency and dynamic load balancing.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Decoupling through client-server allows components to be modified or replaced independently.
Location-transparent applications can migrate to balance load across hardware resources.

architectural_approaches.description: Hardware is designed to be portable between helicopter platforms with every application resident on every box, supporting platform independence.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Portable hardware and standardized interfaces enable easy platform migration and third-party upgrades.
Common architecture across multiple platforms reduces acquisition and sustainment costs.

architectural_approaches.description: Built on commercial standards (ARINC 661, POSIX, CORBA, IEEE P1386/P1386.1, OpenGL, DO 178B) to ensure interoperability and vendor independence.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Standards-based design facilitates third-party integration and future upgrades.
Open standards reduce vendor lock-in and total ownership costs.

children.refinement: System recovers from power interruption within 2 seconds
children.scenario_description: When a 1.9-second power interruption occurs, the system executes a warm boot and is fully operational in 2 seconds.

children.refinement: System continues operation despite partition failure
children.scenario_description: A partition fails, the rest of the processor continues working, and the system continues to function.

children.refinement: Flight-critical data remains available despite hardware damage
children.scenario_description: The data concentrator suffers battle damage, and all flight-critical information is still available.

children.refinement: Display system tolerates mission processor failure
children.scenario_description: The mission processor in the outboard MFD fails, and that display and the rest of the system continue to operate normally.

children.refinement: System handles invalid pilot input without crashing
children.scenario_description: Invalid data is entered by the pilot, and the system does not crash.

children.refinement: System handles invalid bus data without crashing
children.scenario_description: Invalid data comes from an actor on any bus, and the system does not crash.

children.scenario_description: A loss of Doppler occurs, the pilot is notified, and the Doppler timer begins a countdown.

children.refinement: System dynamically balances load across hardware resources

scenarios.scenario_text: When a 1.9-second power interruption occurs, the system executes a warm boot and is fully operational in 2 seconds.
A partition fails, the rest of the processor continues working, and the system continues to function.
The data concentrator suffers battle damage, and all flight-critical information is still available.
The mission processor in the outboard MFD fails, and that display and the rest of the system continue to operate normally.
Invalid data is entered by the pilot, and the system does not crash.
Invalid data comes from an actor on any bus, and the system does not crash.
A loss of Doppler occurs, the pilot is notified, and the Doppler timer begins a countdown.
Under normal operations, no application overruns its processing time limit or memory allotment.
Under high computational load, location-transparent applications migrate to balance processing across available hardware resources without performance degradation.
A new third-party avionics component is integrated into the system within one development cycle without requiring changes to core architecture.
The CAAS architecture is deployed on a new helicopter platform with minimal customization costs.

scenario_reference.text: When a 1.9-second power interruption occurs, the system executes a warm boot and is fully operational in 2 seconds.

analysis_records.response_measure: System fully operational within 2 seconds via warm boot
architectural_decisions: POSIX partitioning maintains state isolation enabling rapid recovery
Layered architecture supports fast warm boot sequences
Critical state persistence mechanisms
analysis_records.reasoning: The partitioned architecture ensures that critical state is isolated and can be quickly restored. The 2-second recovery window requires efficient boot sequences and minimal inter-partition dependencies during startup.

risks.description: Boot sequence timing may exceed 2 seconds under worst-case partition initialization scenarios
risks.rationale: If multiple partitions require sequential initialization with dependencies, cumulative boot time could violate the 2-second constraint

risks.description: State persistence mechanisms may fail during abrupt power loss
risks.rationale: Incomplete writes to non-volatile storage during the 1.9-second window could corrupt critical state data

non_risks.description: Partition isolation prevents cascading boot failures
non_risks.rationale: POSIX partitioning ensures one partition's boot failure does not delay others, supporting parallel recovery

conflicting_attributes: Availability (positive: more frequent saves reduce data loss)
Performance (negative: frequent writes increase overhead)

scenario_reference.text: A partition fails, the rest of the processor continues working, and the system continues to function.

analysis_records.response_measure: Other partitions and system continue normal operation
architectural_decisions: POSIX partitioning creates inviolable timing and memory walls
Fault isolation through strict partition boundaries
No shared state between critical partitions
analysis_records.reasoning: The partitioned architecture's memory and timing walls prevent fault propagation. Each partition operates independently, so a failure in one cannot corrupt or halt others.

risks.description: Inter-partition communication failures may cascade if not properly handled
risks.rationale: If healthy partitions depend on data from the failed partition without timeout/fallback mechanisms, they may stall

risks.description: Shared hardware resources (buses, I/O) may become bottlenecks or single points of failure
risks.rationale: While partitions are isolated in software, hardware resource contention or failure could affect multiple partitions

non_risks.description: Memory corruption in one partition cannot affect others
non_risks.rationale: Inviolable memory walls enforced by POSIX partitioning prevent cross-partition memory access

non_risks.description: Timing overruns in failed partition do not delay other partitions
non_risks.rationale: Timing walls ensure each partition has guaranteed CPU time slots independent of others

conflicting_attributes: Availability (positive: stricter isolation improves fault containment)
Performance (negative: stricter isolation reduces resource sharing efficiency)

scenario_reference.text: The data concentrator suffers battle damage, and all flight-critical information is still available.

architectural_decisions: Redundant data paths and hardware components
Plug-and-play hardware architecture with every application resident on every box
Client-server pattern enabling location transparency
analysis_records.reasoning: The plug-and-play architecture ensures critical applications are replicated across hardware. Location transparency allows clients to access services from alternate hardware when primary sources fail.

risks.description: Network bandwidth saturation during failover may delay critical data delivery
risks.rationale: Rerouting data through alternate paths may exceed available bus bandwidth, causing latency spikes

risks.description: Incomplete data replication may leave gaps in flight-critical information
risks.rationale: If synchronization between redundant components lags, failover may expose stale or missing data

non_risks.description: Application redundancy ensures service availability despite hardware loss
non_risks.rationale: Every application resident on every box means no single hardware failure eliminates a critical service

conflicting_attributes: Availability (positive: more redundancy improves fault tolerance)
Affordability (negative: more redundancy increases hardware costs)

scenario_reference.text: The mission processor in the outboard MFD fails, and that display and the rest of the system continue to operate normally.

analysis_records.stimulus: Mission processor failure in outboard MFD
architectural_decisions: Layered architecture isolates display services from mission processing
Client-server decoupling between display and processing components
Partition-based fault isolation
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered and client-server architecture decouples display rendering from mission processing. The display can continue operating with degraded functionality or by connecting to alternate processors.

risks.description: Display may show stale or incorrect information if failover logic is incomplete
risks.rationale: Without proper detection and notification of processor failure, the display might render outdated mission data

non_risks.description: Display rendering is independent of mission processor health
non_risks.rationale: Layered architecture ensures display services operate autonomously from mission processing logic

non_risks.description: Other system components are unaffected by localized MFD processor failure
non_risks.rationale: Partition isolation and decoupling prevent failure propagation beyond the affected MFD

sensitivity_points.parameter: Failure detection timeout for mission processor

conflicting_attributes: Availability (positive: looser coupling improves fault isolation)
Performance (negative: looser coupling may increase latency)

scenario_reference.text: Invalid data is entered by the pilot, and the system does not crash.

architectural_decisions: Input validation at application layer boundaries
Partition isolation prevents invalid data from corrupting other partitions
Standards-based interfaces (ARINC 661) enforce data contracts
analysis_records.reasoning: Layered architecture and standards-based interfaces enable robust input validation. Partition isolation ensures that even if one application mishandles input, it cannot crash other partitions.

risks.description: Incomplete input validation may allow malformed data to propagate
risks.rationale: If validation logic has gaps, invalid data could reach internal components causing undefined behavior

non_risks.description: Partition boundaries contain any crash to the affected application
non_risks.rationale: Even if invalid input crashes one partition, POSIX partitioning prevents system-wide failure

non_risks.description: Standards-based interfaces provide well-defined input contracts
non_risks.rationale: ARINC 661 and other standards specify valid input ranges and formats, simplifying validation

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: stricter validation prevents invalid data)
Performance (negative: extensive validation increases processing overhead)

scenario_reference.text: Invalid data comes from an actor on any bus, and the system does not crash.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with external bus communication
analysis_records.stimulus: Invalid data from external bus actor
architectural_decisions: Bus interface validation and sanitization
Partition isolation limits blast radius of invalid bus data
Standards-based bus protocols (IEEE P1386) with error detection
analysis_records.reasoning: Standards-based bus protocols include error detection mechanisms. Partition isolation ensures invalid data received by one partition cannot crash others. Interface layers validate and sanitize bus data before internal use.

risks.description: Bus protocol error detection may miss certain malformed messages
risks.rationale: If bus standards do not cover all edge cases, some invalid data may pass through to applications

risks.description: Shared bus resources may be disrupted by misbehaving actors
risks.rationale: A faulty bus actor could flood the bus, causing denial of service even if data is invalid

non_risks.description: Partition isolation contains failures from invalid bus data
non_risks.rationale: Even if invalid bus data crashes a receiving partition, other partitions remain operational

non_risks.description: Standards-based protocols provide built-in error detection
non_risks.rationale: IEEE P1386 and similar standards include checksums and framing to detect corrupted messages

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: deeper validation catches more errors)
Performance (negative: validation adds latency to bus communication)

scenario_reference.text: A loss of Doppler occurs, the pilot is notified, and the Doppler timer begins a countdown.

analysis_records.response_measure: Pilot notification and countdown timer activation
architectural_decisions: Sensor health monitoring at application layer
Event notification through layered architecture
Graceful degradation mechanisms
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered architecture supports sensor health monitoring services that detect failures and trigger notifications. The system continues operating with degraded capability rather than failing completely.

risks.description: Notification delay may leave pilot unaware of sensor failure
risks.rationale: If health monitoring has long polling intervals, the pilot may not be notified promptly

non_risks.description: System continues operation despite sensor loss
non_risks.rationale: Graceful degradation design allows flight to continue with reduced sensor input

non_risks.description: Layered architecture isolates sensor failure from other subsystems
non_risks.rationale: Sensor interface layer prevents Doppler failure from affecting unrelated components

sensitivity_points.parameter: Notification latency from detection to pilot display

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: frequent checks detect failures faster)
Performance (negative: frequent checks consume CPU cycles)

scenario_reference.text: Under normal operations, no application overruns its processing time limit or memory allotment.

analysis_records.response_measure: No timing or memory constraint violations
architectural_decisions: POSIX partitioning enforces timing and memory walls
Static resource allocation per partition
DO 178B compliance ensures predictable execution
analysis_records.reasoning: POSIX partitioning provides hard real-time guarantees by allocating fixed time slices and memory regions to each partition. Applications cannot exceed their allocations, preventing resource contention.

risks.description: Static resource allocation may be inefficient under varying load
risks.rationale: Fixed allocations cannot adapt to dynamic workload changes, potentially wasting resources or causing artificial constraints

risks.description: Worst-case execution time estimates may be overly conservative
risks.rationale: If WCET analysis is too pessimistic, partitions may be over-allocated, reducing overall system capacity

non_risks.description: Timing walls guarantee each partition receives its allocated CPU time
non_risks.rationale: POSIX partitioning enforces temporal isolation, preventing one partition from starving others

non_risks.description: Memory walls prevent applications from exceeding allocated memory
non_risks.rationale: Hardware-enforced memory protection prevents overruns and ensures predictable memory usage

conflicting_attributes: Performance (positive: finer granularity improves utilization)
Reliability (negative: finer granularity increases scheduling complexity and potential for errors)

scenario_reference.text: Under high computational load, location-transparent applications migrate to balance processing across available hardware resources without performance degradation.

analysis_records.response_measure: Dynamic load balancing without performance degradation
architectural_decisions: Client-server pattern with location transparency
Plug-and-play hardware with every application on every box
Dynamic service discovery and binding
analysis_records.reasoning: The client-server architecture with location transparency allows applications to migrate between hardware resources. The plug-and-play design ensures all hardware can host any application, enabling dynamic load balancing.

risks.description: Migration overhead may cause transient performance degradation
risks.rationale: State transfer and service rebinding during migration consume network bandwidth and CPU cycles

risks.description: Load balancing decisions may be suboptimal without global system view
risks.rationale: Distributed load balancing algorithms may make locally optimal but globally suboptimal migration decisions

risks.description: Network bandwidth may become bottleneck during migration
risks.rationale: Simultaneous migrations or large state transfers could saturate bus bandwidth

non_risks.rationale: Every application resident on every box means no code deployment needed during migration

conflicting_attributes: Performance (positive: aggressive migration balances load quickly)
Reliability (negative: frequent migrations increase risk of transient failures)
Modifiability (positive: location transparency enables flexible deployment)
Performance (negative: indirection adds latency to service calls)

scenario_reference.text: A new third-party avionics component is integrated into the system within one development cycle without requiring changes to core architecture.

analysis_records.response_measure: Integration completed within one development cycle without core architecture changes
architectural_decisions: Standards-based open architecture (ARINC 661, POSIX, CORBA)
Layered architecture with well-defined interfaces
Client-server decoupling
analysis_records.reasoning: Standards-based interfaces provide stable integration points. The layered architecture isolates new components from core system logic. Client-server decoupling allows new services to be added without modifying existing clients.

risks.description: Third-party component may not fully comply with standards
risks.rationale: Vendor interpretation of standards may differ, requiring integration workarounds

risks.description: Integration testing may reveal unforeseen interface incompatibilities
risks.rationale: Standards cover functional interfaces but may miss non-functional aspects like timing or resource usage

non_risks.rationale: ARINC 661, CORBA, and other standards provide well-documented, stable integration points

non_risks.description: Layered architecture isolates new components from core system
non_risks.rationale: New components integrate at defined layer boundaries without affecting internal layers

conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (positive: higher abstraction simplifies integration)
Performance (negative: abstraction layers add overhead)

scenario_reference.text: The CAAS architecture is deployed on a new helicopter platform with minimal customization costs.

architectural_decisions: Plug-and-play hardware architecture portable across platforms
Standards-based open architecture reduces vendor lock-in
Common architecture across multiple platforms
analysis_records.reasoning: The plug-and-play hardware design and standards-based interfaces enable platform portability. Reusing a common architecture across platforms amortizes development costs and reduces per-platform customization.

risks.description: Platform-specific hardware interfaces may require custom adapters
risks.rationale: Despite plug-and-play design, physical connectors or bus protocols may differ between platforms

risks.description: Certification costs may not scale linearly with reuse
risks.rationale: Each platform may require separate DO 178B certification, reducing cost savings

non_risks.description: Common architecture reduces development and training costs
non_risks.rationale: Reusing architecture across platforms eliminates redundant design and reduces learning curve

non_risks.description: Standards-based design reduces vendor lock-in and procurement costs
non_risks.rationale: Open standards enable competitive bidding and third-party component sourcing

conflicting_attributes: Affordability (positive: general architecture maximizes reuse)
Performance (negative: platform-specific optimizations may be sacrificed)
